output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.4, "R1.3": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.9}}

Revised the section to remove pervasive hedging, tighten claims to what the reported evidence supports, and replace vague qualifiers with concrete specifics (or explicit gaps where numbers are missing).

Main issues I addressed:

1. **Excessive hedging that weakens factual claims.** Phrases like "appears to perform reasonably well," "seemed to work fairly well," "tentatively interpret as a reasonable result," and "possibly promising direction" hedge statements that are either factual (architecture choices, WER numbers) or should be stated plainly. Hedging belongs on interpretation, not on reporting what was done and measured.
2. **Missing concrete values.** The peak learning rate is described only as one that "seemed to work" — the actual number must appear. Same for warmup schedule, batch size, and SpecAugment policy details.
3. **Unsupported comparative claims.** "Roughly comparable to or perhaps slightly better than several recently reported numbers" cites nothing. Either name the baselines and their WERs or drop the comparison.
4. **Ablation reported imprecisely.** "Approximately 0.6 WER points" of degradation needs a baseline (clean? other? both?) and a single number, not a hedged one.
5. **Conclusion adds no information.** The final sentence ("possibly promising direction that ... may merit additional investigation") should be cut or replaced with a specific next step.

Revised text:

---

This section describes the acoustic modeling component of our end-to-end speech recognition system. The system takes 80-dimensional log-mel filterbank features extracted from 16 kHz audio and produces a sequence of subword tokens. We trained on LibriSpeech (960 h) combined with an internal read-speech corpus of approximately 2,400 hours.

The encoder is a 12-layer Conformer with model dimension 512 and 8 attention heads. The decoder is a 6-layer Transformer. We trained for 200 epochs with AdamW (peak learning rate [VALUE], [N]-step warmup, linear decay; batch size [VALUE]). We did not perform an exhaustive sweep over decoder depth or optimizer hyperparameters.

On LibriSpeech test-clean our system reaches 3.1% WER; on test-other it reaches 7.4%. For reference, [BASELINE A] reports [X]% / [Y]% and [BASELINE B] reports [X]% / [Y]% on the same splits. Our test-other result is within [N] points of the best published number we are aware of; we do not claim a state-of-the-art improvement.

Disabling SpecAugment degrades test-other WER by 0.6 points (from 7.4% to 8.0%) and test-clean by [VALUE] points, confirming that augmentation is a meaningful contributor under our training recipe. We did not isolate the contribution of individual SpecAugment parameters (time-mask width, frequency-mask width, number of masks).

---

Uncertainty: bracketed placeholders mark facts I do not have from the original text (learning rate, warmup, batch size, baseline WERs, test-clean SpecAugment delta). These must be filled in from your actual experiments before submission. The "(from 7.4% to 8.0%)" inference assumes the 0.6-point degradation is on test-other — verify against your ablation logs.
